title_en: Taharah: Maliki Purification Law in Al-Sharh al-Kabir
content_en:
Ad-Dardiri's treatment of taharah (purification) in Al-Sharh al-Kabir follows the text of Mukhtasar Khalil closely while providing the explanations and qualifications needed to apply its terse rulings correctly. The Maliki school's approach to purification has a number of distinctive features that set it apart from the other three major schools.

The Maliki school divides water into two primary categories: pure and purifying water (tahir mutahhir), and impure water (najis) that has been contaminated by physical filth. Unlike the Shafi'i school, the Maliki school does not recognize a distinct category of 'used' (musta'mal) water as legally impure; water that has been used to remove ritual impurity retains its purifying capacity provided it has not been physically contaminated. This is a practically significant difference: a Maliki who washes for wudu' can collect and reuse that water for further washing in a way a Shafi'i jurist would not permit.

A small volume of water (less than two qullahs in Shafi'i terms) becomes ritually impure if any physical filth falls into it and alters one of its three qualities — color, taste, or smell. The Maliki school's position on water of large volume is that it does not become impure unless one of these three qualities is actually altered by the contamination, regardless of the quantity of contaminant. This is more lenient than the Shafi'i position and aligns with common sense experience of large bodies of water.

The Maliki school requires dalk (rubbing) as an integral part of both wudu' and ghusl. This means that merely running water over the limbs is insufficient; the person performing ablution must actively rub the skin. This is a distinctively Maliki requirement not shared by the Hanafi or Shafi'i schools, which do not require rubbing. Ad-Dardiri explains this position in Al-Sharh al-Kabir with reference to the established practice ('amal) of Medina and the interpretive principle that washing (ghasl) requires active contact, not passive wetting.

The Maliki school holds that wiping the entire head (mash al-ra's) is obligatory in wudu', not merely part of it as the Shafi'i school holds. This is derived from the Maliki interpretation of the Quranic verse (5:6) regarding ablution and is consistent with the practice narrated from Imam Malik. Ad-Dardiri presents this ruling as one of the obligatory elements (fara'id) of wudu' in Al-Sharh al-Kabir.

With respect to wiping (mash) over leather socks (khuffayn), the Maliki school permits this concession in principle but restricts it to travelers — resident persons are not permitted to wipe over their socks as a substitute for washing the feet according to the predominant Maliki position. This is more restrictive than the Hanafi and Shafi'i schools, which allow wiping for both travelers and residents (with different time limits). Ad-Dardiri's commentary notes the evidence for the Maliki restriction and its basis in the school's approach to prophetic reports.

Concerning ritual impurity (najasah), the Maliki school holds that dog saliva does not require the sevenfold washing required by the Shafi'i school. A Maliki washing a container licked by a dog washes it until the physical traces of the impurity are gone — the same standard as for any other impurity. This reflects a difference in the Maliki school's understanding of the relevant prophetic reports.

Ad-Dardiri's exposition of taharah in Al-Sharh al-Kabir provides a model of how Maliki scholarship approached the text of Mukhtasar Khalil: respectful of Khalil's authority, attentive to the views of earlier Maliki masters, and careful to apply the school's distinctive methodological principles to resolve apparent tensions in the transmitted positions.
